facts.text: The NBA was founded on June 6, 1946, as the Basketball Association of America (BAA) before merging with the National Basketball League (NBL) in 1949 to form the NBA.
The first NBA game was played on November 1, 1946, when the New York Knickerbockers defeated the Toronto Huskies 68–66 at Maple Leaf Gardens.
The Boston Celtics hold the record for the most NBA championships with 17 titles, followed closely by the Los Angeles Lakers with 17.
Michael Jordan is widely considered the greatest basketball player of all time. He won six NBA championships with the Chicago Bulls and earned five MVP awards.
LeBron James is the NBA's all-time leading scorer, surpassing Kareem Abdul-Jabbar's long-standing record of 38,387 points in February 2023.
Wilt Chamberlain scored 100 points in a single game on March 2, 1962, playing for the Philadelphia Warriors against the New York Knicks. No player has come close to breaking this record.
The NBA's shot clock was introduced in 1954 at 24 seconds, a rule change credited with saving the league by increasing the pace of play and preventing teams from stalling.
Kareem Abdul-Jabbar played 20 NBA seasons and perfected the skyhook, one of the most unstoppable shots in basketball history.
The three-point line was adopted by the NBA for the 1979–80 season, borrowed from the ABA which had used it since 1967.
Magic Johnson and Larry Bird are credited with saving the NBA in the 1980s. Their rivalry, which began in the 1979 NCAA championship game, brought unprecedented attention to the league.
The Chicago Bulls won six NBA championships in eight years during the 1990s, achieving two separate three-peats (1991–93 and 1996–98) under coach Phil Jackson.
The 1995–96 Chicago Bulls went 72–10 in the regular season, a record that stood for 20 years until the 2015–16 Golden State Warriors went 73–9.
Bill Russell won 11 NBA championships in 13 seasons with the Boston Celtics, the most by any player in league history. He also served as a player-coach for his final three seasons.
The NBA Draft Lottery was introduced in 1985 to determine the order of selection for non-playoff teams and prevent teams from intentionally losing to get the top pick.
Kobe Bryant spent his entire 20-year NBA career with the Los Angeles Lakers, winning five championships and earning 18 All-Star selections.
Kobe Bryant scored 81 points against the Toronto Raptors on January 22, 2006, the second-highest single-game point total in NBA history behind Wilt Chamberlain's 100.
The NBA originally had 11 teams in its inaugural 1946–47 season. Only three of those original teams still exist: the Knicks, Celtics, and Warriors.
Shaquille O'Neal was so physically dominant that opposing teams employed a strategy called 'Hack-a-Shaq,' intentionally fouling him because of his poor free-throw shooting.
Tim Duncan is known as 'The Big Fundamental' for his mastery of basic basketball skills. He won five championships with the San Antonio Spurs and was a 15-time All-Star.
Stephen Curry revolutionized basketball with his three-point shooting. He holds the NBA record for most career three-pointers made and most threes in a single season (402 in 2015–16).
The NBA's salary cap was first introduced in the 1984–85 season at $3.6 million per team. By the 2023–24 season, it had grown to over $136 million.
Oscar Robertson averaged a triple-double for the entire 1961–62 season with the Cincinnati Royals, a feat not replicated until Russell Westbrook did it in 2016–17.

Jerry West is the inspiration for the NBA logo, which features a silhouette of a player dribbling. The logo was designed by Alan Siegel in 1969, though the NBA has never officially confirmed it depicts West.
The Boston Celtics won eight consecutive NBA championships from 1959 to 1966, the longest championship streak in NBA history and in any major North American professional sport.
Hakeem Olajuwon led the Houston Rockets to back-to-back championships in 1994 and 1995, the only titles won during Michael Jordan's brief retirement from basketball.
The NBA expanded from 23 teams to 30 between 1988 and 2004, adding teams like the Orlando Magic, Minnesota Timberwolves, Toronto Raptors, and Charlotte Hornets.
Kevin Durant has won two NBA championships and two Finals MVP awards with the Golden State Warriors. He is one of the most prolific scorers in NBA history.
Julius Erving, known as Dr. J, popularized the slam dunk and above-the-rim style of play. He was a star in both the ABA and NBA and won three scoring titles.
The ABA (American Basketball Association) existed from 1967 to 1976 and introduced the three-point line, the slam dunk contest, and the red-white-and-blue basketball.
When the ABA merged with the NBA in 1976, four ABA teams were absorbed: the San Antonio Spurs, Denver Nuggets, Indiana Pacers, and New York Nets.
Charles Barkley was named the NBA MVP in 1993 while playing for the Phoenix Suns. Despite never winning a championship, he is regarded as one of the greatest power forwards ever.
Karl Malone, 'The Mailman,' is the second-leading scorer in NBA history with 36,928 points. He won two MVP awards with the Utah Jazz but never won a championship.
John Stockton holds the NBA records for most career assists (15,806) and most career steals (3,265). Both records are considered virtually unbreakable.
The NBA All-Star Game has been played annually since 1951. The first game was held at the Boston Garden, where the East defeated the West 111–94.
The NBA Slam Dunk Contest began in 1984 and has featured iconic performances, including Michael Jordan's free-throw line dunk in 1988 and Vince Carter's legendary display in 2000.
Allen Iverson, standing just 6 feet tall, won four scoring titles and the 2001 MVP award. His crossover dribble is considered one of the most devastating moves in NBA history.
Iverson famously crossed over Michael Jordan in his rookie season during a 1997 game, a moment that became one of the most replayed highlights in basketball history.
Dwyane Wade won his first NBA championship with the Miami Heat in 2006, earning Finals MVP in his third season. He later won two more titles alongside LeBron James.
Dirk Nowitzki revolutionized the power forward position with his shooting ability. He led the Dallas Mavericks to their only championship in 2011, earning Finals MVP.
Kevin Garnett won the NBA MVP award in 2004 and later won a championship with the Boston Celtics in 2008. His intensity and versatility redefined what a big man could do.
Giannis Antetokounmpo, the 'Greek Freak,' went from the 15th pick in the 2013 draft to winning back-to-back MVP awards in 2019 and 2020, plus the 2021 NBA championship and Finals MVP.
Giannis scored 50 points in Game 6 of the 2021 NBA Finals to clinch the championship for the Milwaukee Bucks, their first title in 50 years.
Luka Doncic was drafted 3rd overall in 2018 after a stellar career with Real Madrid. He won Rookie of the Year and quickly established himself as one of the NBA's brightest stars.
Nikola Jokic, a second-round pick (41st overall) in the 2014 NBA Draft, became one of the most unlikely back-to-back-to-back MVP winners in NBA history (2021, 2022, 2024).
Nikola Jokic led the Denver Nuggets to their first NBA championship in 2023, earning Finals MVP with dominant all-around performances throughout the playoffs.
The Lakers vs. Celtics rivalry is the most storied in NBA history. The two franchises have met in the NBA Finals 12 times, with Boston winning nine of those meetings.
The Bird vs. Magic rivalry defined the 1980s NBA. They met in the Finals three times (1984, 1985, 1987), with Magic's Lakers winning two of the three series.
Phil Jackson is the most successful coach in NBA history with 11 championships — six with the Chicago Bulls and five with the Los Angeles Lakers.
Red Auerbach coached the Boston Celtics to nine NBA championships and later served as team president. He was famous for lighting a victory cigar on the bench when a win was assured.
Pat Riley coached four different teams to the NBA Finals and won five championships — four as head coach of the Lakers and one as president of the Heat.
Scottie Pippen was Michael Jordan's essential partner on the Chicago Bulls dynasty, making six All-Star teams and being named one of the 50 Greatest Players in NBA History.
The 'Bad Boy' Detroit Pistons won back-to-back championships in 1989 and 1990, known for their extremely physical and aggressive style of play called the 'Jordan Rules.'
The NBA introduced the zone defense ban in 2001–02, allowing teams to play zone for the first time since the league's early years, fundamentally changing defensive strategy.
The 24-second shot clock was invented by Danny Biasone, owner of the Syracuse Nationals. He calculated it by dividing 2,880 seconds in a 48-minute game by 120, the average number of shots.
Wilt Chamberlain once averaged 48.5 minutes per game over an entire season in 1961–62, meaning he played every minute of every game including overtimes.
Wilt Chamberlain holds the NBA single-game rebounding record with 55 rebounds, set on November 24, 1960.
The NBA's tallest player ever was Gheorghe Muresan at 7 feet 7 inches. He played for the Washington Bullets and New Jersey Nets in the 1990s.
Muggsy Bogues, at 5 feet 3 inches, is the shortest player in NBA history. He played 14 seasons and was known for his quickness and defensive ability.
The Harlem Globetrotters were founded in 1926 and have entertained fans worldwide with their combination of basketball skill and comedy, though they are not part of the NBA.
Basketball was invented by Dr. James Naismith in December 1891 in Springfield, Massachusetts. He created the game to keep students active during winter, using a soccer ball and peach baskets.
The original 13 rules of basketball, written by James Naismith, were auctioned for $4.3 million in 2010, making them one of the most expensive pieces of sports memorabilia ever sold.
The first basketball game was played with nine players on each side and a soccer ball. The final score was 1–0, with the only goal scored from 25 feet away.
Peach baskets were used as the original goals in basketball. Someone had to climb a ladder to retrieve the ball after each made basket until someone thought to cut the bottom out.
The NBA adopted the current 94-by-50-foot court dimensions and 10-foot-high basket, which has remained the standard since the sport's early years.
A regulation NBA basketball is 29.5 inches in circumference and weighs approximately 22 ounces. The WNBA uses a slightly smaller ball at 28.5 inches.
The backboard was originally introduced not to help with bank shots, but to prevent fans sitting in balconies from interfering with shots at the basket.
The NBA basketball court is made of maple hardwood, specifically sugar maple. The most famous court, the original Boston Garden, had its parquet floor made from scrap wood after World War II.
The free-throw line is 15 feet from the backboard in the NBA. Free throws are worth one point each and are awarded after certain types of fouls.
The key, or paint, was widened from 6 feet to 12 feet in 1951 and then to 16 feet in 1964, largely to limit the dominance of tall players like George Mikan and Wilt Chamberlain.
The Golden State Warriors hold the record for the best regular-season record in NBA history at 73–9 during the 2015–16 season, though they lost the Finals that year.
The 2015–16 Warriors blew a 3–1 lead in the NBA Finals to the Cleveland Cavaliers, giving LeBron James and Cleveland their first major sports championship in 52 years.
Ray Allen's three-pointer in Game 6 of the 2013 NBA Finals is considered one of the greatest shots in NBA history, saving the Miami Heat from elimination against the Spurs.
Michael Jordan's 'flu game' in the 1997 NBA Finals saw him score 38 points while visibly ill, helping the Bulls defeat the Jazz in Game 5 of the Finals.
Michael Jordan hit the game-winning shot in Game 6 of the 1998 NBA Finals against the Utah Jazz, his final shot as a Bull, to clinch his sixth championship.
LeBron James is the only player in NBA history to win championships with three different franchises: the Miami Heat (2012, 2013), Cleveland Cavaliers (2016), and Los Angeles Lakers (2020).
The 2004 NBA Finals is considered one of the biggest upsets in Finals history, as the Detroit Pistons defeated the heavily favored Los Angeles Lakers in five games.
The NBA's longest game was played on January 6, 1951, between the Indianapolis Olympians and the Rochester Royals, lasting six overtimes.
Willis Reed's inspirational appearance in Game 7 of the 1970 NBA Finals, where he played despite a torn thigh muscle, is one of the most iconic moments in NBA history.
The 'Malice at the Palace' brawl in 2004 between the Indiana Pacers and Detroit Pistons fans resulted in some of the longest suspensions in NBA history, including Ron Artest's 86-game ban.
Reggie Miller scored eight points in 8.9 seconds against the New York Knicks in the 1995 playoffs, one of the most remarkable comebacks in NBA postseason history.
Tracy McGrady scored 13 points in 35 seconds against the San Antonio Spurs on December 9, 2004, completing one of the most improbable comebacks in NBA regular-season history.
Magic Johnson announced he was HIV-positive on November 7, 1991, and immediately retired from basketball. He later made a brief comeback and played in the 1992 All-Star Game, winning MVP.
The 1992 United States men's Olympic basketball team, known as the 'Dream Team,' featured Michael Jordan, Magic Johnson, and Larry Bird, and is considered the greatest sports team ever assembled.
The Dream Team won the gold medal at the 1992 Barcelona Olympics by an average margin of 43.8 points, never facing a serious challenge from any opponent.
Basketball became an official Olympic sport at the 1936 Berlin Games. The United States has won the most Olympic gold medals in men's basketball.

The United States men's basketball team suffered a controversial loss to the Soviet Union in the 1972 Olympic gold medal game, a result that American players protested by refusing their silver medals.
Spain, Argentina, and Yugoslavia (and its successor states like Serbia) have been among the strongest international basketball nations, regularly challenging USA Basketball.

FIBA (International Basketball Federation) governs international basketball and was founded in 1932. FIBA rules differ from NBA rules in areas like court size, game length, and the three-point distance.
The FIBA three-point line is 22 feet 1.75 inches from the basket, shorter than the NBA's 23 feet 9 inches. FIBA games are four 10-minute quarters versus the NBA's four 12-minute quarters.
Dirk Nowitzki's 2011 championship run with the Dallas Mavericks is considered one of the greatest individual playoff performances, as he led his team past the Lakers, Thunder, and Heat.
The WNBA was founded in 1996 and began play in 1997 with eight teams. It has grown to become the premier professional women's basketball league in the world.
Diana Taurasi is the WNBA's all-time leading scorer. Known as the 'White Mamba,' she has won three WNBA championships and five Olympic gold medals.

Sue Bird played her entire 21-year WNBA career with the Seattle Storm, winning four championships. She also won five Olympic gold medals and is considered one of the greatest point guards in women's basketball history.
Lisa Leslie became the first player to dunk in a WNBA game on July 30, 2002, a milestone moment for women's basketball.
The Houston Comets won the first four WNBA championships from 1997 to 2000, led by Cynthia Cooper. The franchise folded in 2008.
A'ja Wilson has established herself as one of the WNBA's biggest stars, winning multiple MVP awards and leading the Las Vegas Aces to back-to-back championships in 2022 and 2023.
Caitlin Clark became the NCAA Division I all-time leading scorer (men's or women's) during her career at Iowa, surpassing Pete Maravich's long-standing record.
Caitlin Clark was the No. 1 overall pick in the 2024 WNBA Draft by the Indiana Fever, sparking unprecedented interest and attendance in the WNBA.
The NCAA Men's Basketball Tournament, known as March Madness, is one of the most popular sporting events in the United States, with millions of fans filling out brackets each year.
The term 'March Madness' was first used by an Illinois high school official in 1939 and was later adopted for the NCAA tournament. CBS broadcaster Brent Musburger helped popularize it nationally.
The NCAA tournament expanded to 64 teams in 1985 (now 68 with the First Four), creating the modern bracket format that has become a cultural phenomenon.
A perfect March Madness bracket has odds of approximately 1 in 9.2 quintillion. No one has ever been verified to have picked a perfect bracket.
The biggest upset in March Madness history is widely considered to be No. 16 seed UMBC defeating No. 1 seed Virginia 74–54 in 2018, the first time a 16 seed beat a 1 seed.
UCLA holds the record for the most NCAA men's basketball championships with 11 titles, including seven consecutive championships from 1967 to 1973 under coach John Wooden.
John Wooden's UCLA dynasty is the most dominant in college basketball history. His teams once won 88 consecutive games, a record that still stands.
Duke University and coach Mike Krzyzewski (Coach K) won five NCAA championships together. Coach K retired in 2022 as the winningest coach in Division I men's basketball history.
The Final Four, the semifinal round of the NCAA tournament, has been held annually since 1939 and regularly draws crowds of over 70,000 to massive domed stadiums.
Christian Laettner's buzzer-beating turnaround jumper for Duke against Kentucky in the 1992 NCAA Tournament Elite Eight is often called the greatest game in college basketball history.
NC State's upset of Houston's 'Phi Slama Jama' squad in the 1983 NCAA championship game, capped by Lorenzo Charles's buzzer-beating dunk, is one of March Madness's most iconic moments.
Villanova's 1985 NCAA championship victory over Georgetown is considered one of the greatest upsets in tournament history. The Wildcats shot 78.6% from the field in the title game.
The NBA Draft has been held annually since 1947 and currently consists of two rounds. Top draft picks often become franchise cornerstones and future Hall of Famers.
The 1984 NBA Draft is considered the greatest draft class ever, producing Hakeem Olajuwon (1st), Michael Jordan (3rd), Charles Barkley (5th), and John Stockton (16th).
The 2003 NBA Draft is another historically great class, featuring LeBron James (1st), Carmelo Anthony (3rd), Chris Bosh (4th), and Dwyane Wade (5th).
Sam Bowie was drafted 2nd overall in 1984 by the Portland Trail Blazers, ahead of Michael Jordan. It is widely regarded as the biggest draft mistake in NBA history.
The NBA's collective bargaining agreement governs player salaries, free agency, and the salary cap. It is renegotiated periodically between the league and the players' union.
Free agency in the NBA began in earnest after the Oscar Robertson suit in 1976, which challenged the reserve clause and gave players greater freedom to choose their teams.
LeBron James's decision to leave Cleveland for Miami in 2010, announced on a live TV special called 'The Decision,' was one of the most controversial moments in NBA free agency history.
Kevin Durant's move to the Golden State Warriors in 2016 was one of the most polarizing free-agency decisions ever, as he joined a team that had just set the regular-season wins record.
The NBA salary cap includes exceptions like the Larry Bird exception, named after the Celtics legend, which allows teams to exceed the cap to re-sign their own players.
The most lopsided trade in NBA history is often considered to be the 1996 draft-day trade that sent Kobe Bryant from the Charlotte Hornets to the Los Angeles Lakers for Vlade Divac.
The Boston Celtics acquired Kevin Garnett and Ray Allen in 2007 trades, forming a 'Big Three' with Paul Pierce that won the 2008 NBA championship.
James Harden was traded from Oklahoma City to Houston in 2012 for what many consider a modest return, and he subsequently became an MVP-caliber player with the Rockets.
AND1 Mixtape Tour was a streetball phenomenon in the early 2000s that showcased flashy dribbling and dunking, popularizing playground basketball culture worldwide.
Rucker Park in Harlem, New York, is the most famous outdoor basketball court in the world. NBA legends like Kareem Abdul-Jabbar, Kobe Bryant, and Kevin Durant have played there.
The Harlem Globetrotters have played over 26,000 games in more than 120 countries, using basketball as entertainment and goodwill ambassadorship around the globe.
Meadowlark Lemon and Curly Neal were two of the most famous Harlem Globetrotters in the team's history, becoming cultural icons beyond basketball.
Michael Jordan's sneaker deal with Nike launched the Air Jordan brand in 1984. The Air Jordan line has generated billions in revenue and remains the most popular basketball shoe brand.
The first Air Jordan sneakers were banned by the NBA for violating the league's uniform policy. Nike paid the $5,000 fine per game and used the ban as a marketing tool.
Converse Chuck Taylor All Stars were the dominant basketball shoe for decades before Nike and other brands took over in the 1970s and 1980s.
Basketball shoes have evolved from simple canvas sneakers to high-tech footwear with air cushioning, carbon fiber plates, and advanced traction patterns.
NBA players' shoe deals can be worth hundreds of millions of dollars. LeBron James signed a lifetime deal with Nike reportedly worth over $1 billion.
The NBA banned the Nike 'Adapt BB' self-lacing shoes briefly in 2019 after Zion Williamson's shoe blew apart during a game, though the issue was with a different Nike model.
Zion Williamson's sneaker blowout during a Duke vs. UNC game in 2019 went viral and raised questions about shoe construction. Nike's stock temporarily dropped after the incident.
The triple-double (double digits in three statistical categories in one game) was not officially tracked by the NBA until the 1979–80 season, though players had achieved them before.
Russell Westbrook holds the NBA record for most career triple-doubles with over 190, surpassing Oscar Robertson's record of 181 in 2021.
A quadruple-double (double digits in four categories) has been officially recorded only four times in NBA history, achieved by Nate Thurmond, Alvin Robertson, Hakeem Olajuwon, and David Robinson.
The NBA's single-season assists record is held by John Stockton, who dished out 1,164 assists during the 1990–91 season, averaging 14.5 per game.
The NBA's single-game assists record is 30, held by Scott Skiles of the Orlando Magic, set on December 30, 1990, against the Denver Nuggets.
Wilt Chamberlain is the only player in NBA history to average more than 50 points per game for an entire season. He averaged 50.4 PPG during the 1961–62 season.
The NBA record for most points in a quarter is 33, shared by George Gervin and Carmelo Anthony.
Mark Eaton holds the NBA single-season blocks record with 456 blocks during the 1984–85 season, averaging 5.56 blocks per game.
Hakeem Olajuwon is the NBA's all-time leader in blocked shots with 3,830 career blocks, significantly ahead of second-place Dikembe Mutombo.
The NBA's longest winning streak is 33 games, set by the Los Angeles Lakers during the 1971–72 season. The team finished with a then-record 69 wins.
The NBA's longest losing streak is 28 games, shared by the Cleveland Cavaliers (2010–11) and the Philadelphia 76ers (2014–15 to 2015–16, spanning two seasons).
The Philadelphia 76ers intentionally lost games as part of 'The Process,' a tanking strategy masterminded by GM Sam Hinkie to accumulate high draft picks and rebuild the franchise.
Wilt Chamberlain never fouled out of a game in his entire NBA career, spanning 1,045 regular-season games.
Dennis Rodman won seven consecutive rebounding titles from 1992 to 1998, despite being only 6'7". His rebounding techniques and intensity were unmatched.
Dennis Rodman was known for his colorful hair, piercings, and outlandish personality off the court, but was a tenacious defender and rebounder who won five championships.
The NBA's Sixth Man of the Year Award has been given annually since 1983. Jamal Crawford won it a record three times.
The NBA Most Improved Player Award was first given in 1986. It recognizes a player who has shown the most improvement from the previous season.
The NBA Defensive Player of the Year Award was first given in 1983. Dikembe Mutombo and Ben Wallace each won it four times.
Dikembe Mutombo was famous for wagging his finger after blocking shots, one of the most iconic celebrations in NBA history.
The NBA introduced the defensive three-second violation in 2001–02, which prevents defensive players from camping in the paint without guarding an opponent.
The NBA's 'hack-a-player' strategy, where teams intentionally foul poor free-throw shooters, led to rule changes in 2016 that penalized intentional fouls in the last two minutes of each quarter.
The NBA's clear path foul rule prevents defenders from fouling offensive players who have a clear path to the basket on a fast break, awarding two free throws and possession.
Goaltending was first made illegal by the NCAA in 1944 and the NBA in 1945, largely in response to George Mikan's ability to swat shots near the basket.
The NBA adopted instant replay for the first time in the 2002–03 season, initially limited to buzzer-beater shots. It has since expanded significantly.
The NBA's Coach's Challenge was introduced in the 2019–20 season, allowing each team one challenge per game to dispute certain referee calls using instant replay.
George Mikan was the NBA's first dominant big man, leading the Minneapolis Lakers to five championships in six years during the late 1940s and early 1950s.
George Mikan was so dominant that the NBA widened the lane from 6 feet to 12 feet, a change often called 'the Mikan rule.'
The Minneapolis Lakers moved to Los Angeles in 1960, a surprising choice since there were no lakes in Los Angeles. The name was retained as a nod to the team's origins in Minnesota.
The Golden State Warriors franchise originated in Philadelphia in 1946 before moving to San Francisco in 1962 and later adopting the 'Golden State' name in 1971.
The Sacramento Kings franchise is the oldest continuously operating professional basketball team in the United States, tracing its history back to the Rochester Royals in 1923.
The Toronto Raptors, founded in 1995, are the only current NBA team based in Canada. They won their first NBA championship in 2019.
Kawhi Leonard's buzzer-beater in Game 7 of the 2019 Eastern Conference Semifinals against the 76ers bounced on the rim four times before going in, one of the most dramatic shots in NBA history.
Kawhi Leonard led the Toronto Raptors to their first NBA championship in 2019, winning Finals MVP, after being traded from San Antonio in 2018.
The San Antonio Spurs dynasty under coach Gregg Popovich won five championships between 1999 and 2014, built around Tim Duncan and later Tony Parker and Manu Ginobili.
Gregg Popovich is the winningest coach in NBA regular-season history, surpassing Don Nelson's record in 2022.
The 'Showtime' Lakers of the 1980s, featuring Magic Johnson, Kareem Abdul-Jabbar, and James Worthy, won five championships with their fast-paced, entertaining style of play.
The 'Big Three' era of the Miami Heat—LeBron James, Dwyane Wade, and Chris Bosh—produced four consecutive Finals appearances and two championships (2012, 2013).
The Golden State Warriors dynasty from 2015 to 2022 won four championships in eight years, led by Stephen Curry, Klay Thompson, and Draymond Green.
Klay Thompson scored 37 points in a single quarter against the Sacramento Kings in 2015, an NBA record for most points in a quarter that stood until surpassed.
Klay Thompson tore his ACL in the 2019 NBA Finals and his Achilles in 2020, missing over two full seasons. He returned and helped the Warriors win the 2022 championship.
Stephen Curry won back-to-back MVP awards in 2015 and 2016, with his 2016 win being the first unanimous MVP selection in NBA history.
The 'Splash Brothers' nickname for Stephen Curry and Klay Thompson refers to their prolific three-point shooting. Together, they are the greatest shooting backcourt in NBA history.
Draymond Green, despite limited scoring, is considered one of the greatest defenders and most versatile players in NBA history, earning four All-Star selections and Defensive Player of the Year.
The NBA plays 82 regular-season games, significantly more than the NFL (17) but fewer than the MLB (162).
NBA teams play four 12-minute quarters for a total of 48 minutes of game time, longer than FIBA's 40 minutes and college basketball's two 20-minute halves.
The NBA playoffs feature 16 teams in a best-of-seven format for each round, typically running from April through June.
The play-in tournament, introduced permanently in 2021, allows the 7th through 10th seeds in each conference to compete for the final two playoff spots.
The NBA Finals MVP award has been given since 1969. The trophy was renamed the Bill Russell NBA Finals MVP Award in 2009 to honor the Celtics legend.
Michael Jordan won six Finals MVP awards, the most in NBA history. He never lost an NBA Finals series, going 6–0 in the championship round.
LeBron James has appeared in 10 NBA Finals, winning four of them, and has been named Finals MVP four times.
The Larry O'Brien Championship Trophy has been awarded to the NBA champion since 1977. It is made of sterling silver, weighs 14.5 pounds, and stands two feet tall.
Before 1977, the NBA championship trophy was known as the Walter A. Brown Trophy, named after the first owner of the Boston Celtics.
The NBA G League (formerly the D-League) serves as the NBA's official minor league, providing development opportunities for players, coaches, and referees.
Many NBA stars spent time in the G League before making it to the NBA, including Pascal Siakam, Fred VanVleet, and Danny Green.
Robert Horry won seven NBA championships, more than any player of his era, earning him the nickname 'Big Shot Rob' for his clutch playoff performances.
Robert Horry hit a series-changing three-pointer in Game 4 of the 2005 NBA Finals for the San Antonio Spurs, one of many clutch shots in his playoff career.
The concept of a player earning a 'supermax' contract was introduced in the 2017 CBA, allowing teams to offer even more money to retain their best players.
LeBron James's career has spanned playing with three different franchises—Cleveland, Miami, and Los Angeles—and he has led all three to the Finals.
Michael Jordan retired from basketball three times: first in 1993 to play baseball, then in 1998 after his sixth championship, and finally in 2003 after playing for the Washington Wizards.
Michael Jordan briefly played minor league baseball for the Birmingham Barons in 1994, hitting .202 in 127 games before returning to basketball.
Michael Jordan's return to the NBA in March 1995 was announced with a two-word press release: 'I'm back.' He helped the Bulls reach the playoffs that year but they fell short of the Finals.
LeBron James was drafted first overall in 2003 out of high school by his hometown Cleveland Cavaliers, following massive hype as 'The Chosen One.'
LeBron James appeared on the cover of Sports Illustrated at age 16 while still in high school, with the headline 'The Chosen One.'
Kobe Bryant was drafted 13th overall by the Charlotte Hornets in 1996 and was immediately traded to the Lakers. He entered the NBA straight from high school at age 17.
Kobe Bryant and Shaquille O'Neal formed one of the most dominant duos in NBA history, winning three consecutive championships with the Lakers from 2000 to 2002.
The Kobe-Shaq feud eventually led to Shaquille O'Neal being traded to the Miami Heat in 2004, ending one of the greatest partnerships in basketball history.
Kobe Bryant's tragic death in a helicopter crash on January 26, 2020, along with his daughter Gianna and seven others, sent shockwaves through the sports world.
The NBA retired the number 6 league-wide in 2022 to honor Bill Russell, who had passed away earlier that year.
The number 23 is associated with both Michael Jordan and LeBron James, the two most prominent players to wear it. Many teams have retired it in Jordan's honor.
Kareem Abdul-Jabbar changed his name from Lew Alcindor after converting to Islam in 1971. He remains one of the most culturally significant athletes in American history.
Kareem Abdul-Jabbar's skyhook was virtually unblockable due to his 7'2" frame and the high release point. Opposing teams couldn't find a reliable way to defend it.
Magic Johnson earned his nickname in high school after a sportswriter saw him record a triple-double. At 6'9", he revolutionized the point guard position.
Magic Johnson's no-look passes and court vision made him one of the most entertaining players ever. He won five championships and three Finals MVP awards with the Lakers.
Larry Bird won three consecutive MVP awards from 1984 to 1986 and three championships with the Boston Celtics. He was known for his trash talk as much as his shooting.
Larry Bird is the only person in NBA history to win MVP, Coach of the Year, and Executive of the Year awards, achieving a unique trifecta in the sport.
Larry Bird and Magic Johnson starred in a Converse commercial together in 1985, which helped humanize their rivalry and build their friendship.
Patrick Ewing was the first overall pick in the 1985 NBA Draft, selected by the New York Knicks. He was an 11-time All-Star but never won an NBA championship.
The 1985 draft lottery, in which the Knicks won the first overall pick and selected Patrick Ewing, has been the subject of conspiracy theories due to a supposedly frozen envelope.
David Robinson, 'The Admiral,' won two championships with the San Antonio Spurs and scored 71 points in a single game on the last day of the 1993–94 season to win the scoring title.
Shaquille O'Neal was one of the most physically dominant players ever at 7'1" and 325 pounds. He won four championships and three consecutive Finals MVPs (2000–02).
Shaquille O'Neal's free-throw shooting was notoriously poor, with a career average of 52.7%. Despite this, he was nearly unstoppable due to his size and skill around the basket.
Yao Ming, selected first overall in the 2002 NBA Draft, was instrumental in growing basketball's popularity in China. His impact on the global game extended far beyond his on-court career.
The NBA has held regular-season games in international locations including London, Paris, Mexico City, and Tokyo, as part of its global expansion efforts.
NBA Africa Game has been played multiple times, showcasing basketball talent from the African continent and growing the sport's popularity across Africa.
Manu Ginobili, from Argentina, is one of only two players to win an NBA championship, an Olympic gold medal, and a EuroLeague title.
Tony Parker, born in Belgium and raised in France, was the first European-born player to be named NBA Finals MVP, winning the award in 2007.
Pau Gasol and his brother Marc Gasol both played in the NBA and are the only brothers to have both won NBA championships.
Marc Gasol won the NBA Defensive Player of the Year award in 2013 and helped the Toronto Raptors win their first championship in 2019.
The EuroLeague is the top-tier professional basketball league in Europe and produces many future NBA players each year.
Luka Doncic won the EuroLeague MVP and championship with Real Madrid at age 19 before entering the 2018 NBA Draft, making him one of the most accomplished European prospects ever.
The NBA's international player pool has grown significantly. In the 2023–24 season, over 120 international players from more than 40 countries were on NBA rosters.
Detlef Schrempf, from Germany, was one of the first European players to have a successful NBA career, paving the way for future international stars like Dirk Nowitzki.
The 2006 FIBA World Championship (now World Cup) was won by Spain, marking a shift in global basketball power away from absolute American dominance.
The United States' 'Redeem Team' won gold at the 2008 Beijing Olympics, reclaiming dominance after disappointing showings in 2002 and 2004.
Carmelo Anthony is the all-time leading scorer in USA Basketball Olympic history, having won three gold medals and one bronze medal across four Olympic Games.
Basketball is the third most popular sport in the world, behind soccer and cricket, with an estimated 2.2 billion fans globally.
China has the largest basketball fan base outside the United States, with hundreds of millions of players and fans, largely due to Yao Ming's influence.
The Philippine Basketball Association (PBA), founded in 1975, is the second-oldest professional basketball league in the world after the NBA.
Lithuania is one of the most basketball-passionate countries in the world relative to its size. Basketball is considered the country's national sport.
The Naismith Memorial Basketball Hall of Fame is located in Springfield, Massachusetts, the birthplace of basketball. It inducts players, coaches, and contributors annually.
Players become eligible for the Basketball Hall of Fame four years after retirement. The Hall includes players from the NBA, WNBA, college, and international basketball.
The NBA Commissioner is the league's chief executive. Adam Silver has held the position since 2014, succeeding David Stern, who led the NBA for 30 years.
David Stern is credited with transforming the NBA from a struggling league in the early 1980s into a global entertainment powerhouse through marketing, expansion, and television deals.
The NBA's first nationally televised game was a 1954 contest between the Boston Celtics and the Knicks. Early TV ratings were poor, and some Finals games aired on tape delay into the 1980s.
NBA Finals games aired on tape delay as late as the early 1980s. CBS moved games to prime time partially due to the Magic vs. Bird rivalry boosting ratings.
The NBA's television deal with ESPN, TNT, and ABC has been worth billions of dollars, making basketball one of the most lucrative sports for broadcast rights.
TNT's 'Inside the NBA' featuring Charles Barkley, Shaquille O'Neal, Ernie Johnson, and Kenny Smith is one of the most popular and entertaining sports studio shows in television history.
Charles Barkley famously declared 'I am not a role model' in a 1993 Nike commercial, sparking a national debate about athletes' responsibilities to society.
The dunk was banned in NCAA basketball from 1967 to 1976, a rule widely seen as targeting Kareem Abdul-Jabbar (then Lew Alcindor) during his dominant college career at UCLA.
The alley-oop, where one player throws the ball near the basket for a teammate to catch and dunk in mid-air, became a signature play of the 'Lob City' Los Angeles Clippers.
The crossover dribble became a cultural phenomenon thanks to Allen Iverson and Tim Hardaway. Hardaway's 'UTEP Two-Step' and Iverson's ankle-breakers are legendary basketball moves.
The Euro step, popularized in the NBA by Manu Ginobili, involves taking two steps in different directions to evade defenders on the way to the basket.
The 'Dream Shake' was Hakeem Olajuwon's signature post move, a series of fakes, spins, and counter-moves that made him virtually unguardable in the low post.
The finger roll, perfected by George Gervin ('The Iceman'), is a layup variation where the ball rolls off the fingertips with a soft touch. Gervin won four scoring titles using it.

Stephen Curry's shooting range extends well beyond the three-point line, regularly making shots from 30+ feet. His deep threes have been called 'logo threes' because he shoots from the center court logo.
The NBA's pace of play has increased dramatically since the mid-2010s, driven by analytics that emphasize three-point shooting and fewer midrange jumpers.
The 'Moreyball' philosophy, named after Houston Rockets GM Daryl Morey, emphasized three-pointers and shots at the rim while nearly eliminating the midrange game.
The corner three-pointer is statistically the most efficient three-point shot because the three-point line is closest to the basket at the corners (22 feet vs. 23 feet 9 inches above the break).
The NBA's average three-point attempts per game have more than doubled since the 2012–13 season, reflecting the analytics revolution in basketball strategy.
Player tracking technology using cameras installed in NBA arenas captures every movement on the court, generating detailed data on speed, distance, and shot quality.
The term 'floor general' refers to a point guard who controls the offense on the court, directing plays and managing tempo. Famous floor generals include Magic Johnson, John Stockton, and Chris Paul.
Chris Paul, despite never winning an NBA championship, is one of the greatest point guards in history with over 11,000 career assists and nine All-NBA selections.
Chris Paul was traded from the New Orleans Hornets to the Los Angeles Clippers in 2011 after the NBA controversially vetoed an earlier trade to the Lakers.
The NBA vetoing the Chris Paul trade to the Lakers in 2011 remains one of the most controversial decisions in league history. The NBA owned the Hornets at the time.
Pete Maravich, known as 'Pistol Pete,' was one of the most creative and entertaining players in basketball history. He averaged 44.2 points per game during his college career at LSU.
Pete Maravich's college scoring records were set without a three-point line and with freshmen being ineligible to play varsity. His records stood for decades.
Elgin Baylor is considered the prototype for the modern small forward. He once scored 71 points in a game and averaged over 38 points per game in a playoff series.
Bob Cousy was the NBA's first great point guard, known for his behind-the-back dribble and flashy passing. He won six championships with the Boston Celtics.
Walt Frazier, 'Clyde,' was the stylish leader of the New York Knicks' two championship teams in 1970 and 1973, known for his defense and clutch play.
Isaiah Thomas (the Detroit Pistons version) led the 'Bad Boy' Pistons to back-to-back titles and scored 25 points in a quarter of the 1988 NBA Finals despite a severely sprained ankle.
Kawhi Leonard is one of the few players to win Finals MVP with two different teams — the Spurs in 2014 and the Raptors in 2019.
Paul Pierce, 'The Truth,' earned his nickname from Shaquille O'Neal. He was Finals MVP when the Celtics won the 2008 championship.
Kevin McHale's Game 4 performance in the 1985 NBA Finals, where he scored 56 points against the Lakers, remains a hallmark of post play excellence.
The 2016 NBA Finals between the Warriors and Cavaliers drew an average of 20.2 million viewers for the series, with Game 7 attracting 31 million, the most-watched NBA game since 1998.
Space Jam (1996), starring Michael Jordan alongside animated Looney Tunes characters, became a cultural phenomenon and remains one of the most iconic basketball movies ever made.
Hoosiers (1986), based on the true story of a small-town Indiana high school basketball team, is widely regarded as one of the greatest sports movies of all time.
He Got Game (1998), directed by Spike Lee and starring Denzel Washington and real-life NBA player Ray Allen, explored the pressures of high school basketball recruiting.
White Men Can't Jump (1992), starring Woody Harrelson and Wesley Snipes, captured the culture of Los Angeles playground basketball and became a beloved sports comedy.
Basketball diaries, both the book by Jim Carroll and the 1995 film starring Leonardo DiCaprio, depicted basketball culture in New York City.
The NBA 2K video game franchise has become a cultural force, generating billions in revenue. Its realistic gameplay and player ratings are taken seriously by actual NBA players.
NBA Jam, the 1993 arcade game, is one of the most popular basketball video games ever made. Its 'He's on fire!' commentary became iconic in gaming culture.
The phrase 'And one!' is shouted when a player makes a basket while being fouled, resulting in a chance for a three-point play (the basket plus a free throw).
A 'poster dunk' refers to a dunk so spectacular that it could be featured on a poster, usually performed over a defender who is posterized.
Vince Carter's dunk over 7'2" French center Frédéric Weis at the 2000 Sydney Olympics is often called the greatest dunk ever, nicknamed 'le dunk de la mort' (the dunk of death).
Vince Carter's performance in the 2000 NBA Slam Dunk Contest is considered the greatest dunk contest performance ever, featuring between-the-legs and 360-degree dunks.
The 'poster' dunk by DeAndre Jordan over Brandon Knight in 2013 is one of the NBA's most replayed highlights, as Knight was completely overwhelmed at the rim.
LeBron James's chasedown block on Andre Iguodala in Game 7 of the 2016 NBA Finals is considered one of the greatest defensive plays in Finals history.
Kareem Abdul-Jabbar won six MVP awards, the most in NBA history, earning them with both the Milwaukee Bucks and the Los Angeles Lakers.

The NBA's luxury tax penalizes teams that spend above a certain threshold, charging them extra dollars for every dollar spent over the tax line. The tax can multiply significantly for repeat offenders.
The NBA draft lottery uses a weighted system where the worst teams have the best odds. In 2019, the odds were flattened so the three worst teams each had a 14% chance at the top pick.
Anthony Davis was traded from the New Orleans Pelicans to the Los Angeles Lakers in 2019 in exchange for a massive package including Lonzo Ball, Brandon Ingram, Josh Hart, and multiple draft picks.
The 'Lob City' Clippers, featuring Chris Paul, Blake Griffin, and DeAndre Jordan, were known for spectacular alley-oops but never advanced past the second round of the playoffs.
Blake Griffin's 'over the car' dunk in the 2011 Slam Dunk Contest is one of the most memorable dunk contest moments, though it was controversial since a KIA car was placed as a prop.
The NBA has a strict drug testing policy. Players can be suspended for violations involving performance-enhancing drugs, recreational drugs, or other banned substances.
The NBA's anti-tampering rules prohibit teams from negotiating with players under contract with other teams. Several teams and executives have been fined for tampering violations.
The minimum age for the NBA draft was set at 19 years old (or one year removed from high school) starting with the 2006 draft, commonly known as the 'one-and-done' rule.
Before the one-and-done rule, players like Kobe Bryant, Kevin Garnett, LeBron James, and Dwight Howard went directly from high school to the NBA.
The one-and-done rule has been debated for years, with many arguing it should be eliminated to allow players to enter the NBA directly from high school again.
Kevin Garnett was the first player drafted out of high school in the modern era (1995), opening the door for future prep-to-pro players like Kobe Bryant and LeBron James.
The NBA's revenue sharing system helps smaller-market teams remain competitive by redistributing a portion of league-wide revenue generated by larger markets.
Madison Square Garden in New York City is often called 'The Mecca of Basketball' and has hosted some of the most memorable games and moments in NBA history.
Staples Center (now Crypto.com Arena) in Los Angeles has been home to both the Lakers and the Clippers, hosting numerous championship celebrations and iconic moments.
The United Center in Chicago replaced the original Chicago Stadium in 1994 and features a statue of Michael Jordan outside, one of the most photographed sports monuments.
TD Garden in Boston, home of the Celtics, continues the tradition of the original Boston Garden, where the team's legendary parquet floor became a symbol of basketball excellence.
The NBA has 30 teams divided into two conferences (Eastern and Western) and six divisions of five teams each.
The Western Conference has historically been considered stronger than the Eastern Conference, though the balance has shifted multiple times throughout NBA history.
James Naismith, the inventor of basketball, is the only Basketball Hall of Fame inductee who is honored as a contributor rather than a player or coach.
James Naismith lived to see basketball become an Olympic sport at the 1936 Berlin Games, where he tossed up the ceremonial first ball.
The first professional basketball league was the National Basketball League (NBL), founded in 1898, decades before the NBA.
The NBA's first African American players were Earl Lloyd, Chuck Cooper, and Nat 'Sweetwater' Clifton, who broke the league's color barrier in 1950.
Earl Lloyd became the first African American to play in an NBA game on October 31, 1950, playing for the Washington Capitols.
Bill Russell was the first Black head coach in NBA history, taking on the player-coach role with the Boston Celtics in 1966.
The NBA is the most diverse major professional sports league in North America, with approximately 70-75% of players being African American.
The NBA players' union, the National Basketball Players Association (NBPA), was founded in 1954 and is one of the oldest labor unions in professional sports.
Oscar Robertson's antitrust lawsuit against the NBA in 1970 eventually led to unrestricted free agency, one of the most important legal victories in professional sports labor history.
The reserve clause, which bound players to their teams indefinitely, was effectively ended by the Robertson settlement in 1976, giving players the right to become free agents.
Bob Pettit was the first player to win the NBA MVP award in 1956 and the first to score 20,000 career points.
The NBA scoring title goes to the player with the highest points per game average, provided they meet minimum games played requirements. It is one of basketball's most prestigious individual honors.
LeBron James won his first scoring title in the 2007–08 season with a 30.0 points per game average. He didn't win another until the 2022–23 season.
Joel Embiid, from Cameroon, was named NBA MVP in 2023, continuing a trend of international players winning the league's top individual award.
Victor Wembanyama was selected first overall in the 2023 NBA Draft by the San Antonio Spurs. At 7'4" with a 8-foot wingspan, he is considered a generational prospect.
The NBA Summer League, held primarily in Las Vegas, gives rookies and young players a chance to showcase their skills before the regular season begins.
NBA teams typically carry 15 players on their roster during the regular season, with 13 active on game nights and two designated as inactive.
The NBA's 10-day contract allows teams to sign players for short stints, providing roster flexibility and giving fringe players opportunities to prove themselves.
Two-way contracts, introduced in 2017, allow teams to have players split time between the NBA and G League, providing a development pathway.
The mid-level exception is a salary cap mechanism that allows teams over the cap to sign free agents for a specified amount, named because it approximates the league's average salary.
Basketball positions have traditionally been numbered 1 through 5: point guard (1), shooting guard (2), small forward (3), power forward (4), and center (5).
'Positionless basketball' has become increasingly common in the modern NBA, where versatile players can play multiple positions and switch on defense.
The 'small ball' revolution involves teams playing without a traditional center, instead using a quicker, more versatile lineup to create matchup advantages.
The Golden State Warriors popularized the 'Death Lineup' (or 'Lineup of Death'), a small-ball unit featuring Draymond Green at center that devastated opposing teams.
A 'stretch four' or 'stretch five' is a power forward or center who can shoot three-pointers, pulling opposing big men away from the basket and creating driving lanes.
The NBA's average player height is approximately 6'6", though this varies by position. Point guards average around 6'2" while centers average around 6'11".
The NBA combine measures players' physical attributes including height (with and without shoes), wingspan, standing reach, vertical leap, lane agility, and three-quarter court sprint.
Manute Bol and Gheorghe Muresan, both 7'7", are tied for the tallest players in NBA history. Bol once hit six three-pointers in a single half despite being primarily a shot-blocker.
Spud Webb, at 5'7", won the 1986 NBA Slam Dunk Contest, proving that height isn't everything when it comes to athleticism.
Nate Robinson, at 5'9", is the only player to win the NBA Slam Dunk Contest three times (2006, 2009, 2010).
The 'Big Man' era of the NBA featured dominant centers like Shaquille O'Neal, Patrick Ewing, Hakeem Olajuwon, and David Robinson battling throughout the 1990s.
David Stern introduced the NBA dress code in 2005, requiring players to wear business casual attire when on team business, a controversial policy at the time.
Pre-game tunnel walks have become a modern NBA fashion runway, with players showcasing designer outfits as they arrive at arenas. Russell Westbrook is known for his bold fashion choices.
The 'And1 Mixtape' video series featured streetball legends like Hot Sauce, Skip to My Lou (Rafer Alston), and The Professor, blending basketball with hip-hop culture.
Rafer Alston, known as 'Skip to My Lou' from the And1 Mixtape Tour, transitioned from streetball fame to a 10-year NBA career, proving streetball skills could translate to the professional level.
The Professor (Grayson Boucher), an And1 Mixtape Tour star, became a YouTube sensation and is one of the most popular basketball content creators in the world.
Street basketball culture has deep roots in New York City, with iconic courts at Rucker Park, 'The Cage' at West 4th Street, and various playgrounds across the five boroughs.
The NBA's relationship with hip-hop culture has been significant since the 1990s, with players like Allen Iverson and Shaquille O'Neal releasing rap albums.
Shaquille O'Neal released four rap albums during his career. His debut album 'Shaq Diesel' went platinum in 1993.
Damian Lillard is an accomplished rapper under the name Dame D.O.L.L.A., releasing multiple albums while maintaining his status as an NBA star.
Damian Lillard hit a series-ending three-pointer from 37 feet against the Oklahoma City Thunder in the 2019 playoffs, waving goodbye to the Thunder as the ball went in.
Damian Lillard's buzzer-beater against the Houston Rockets in the 2014 playoffs was his first iconic postseason moment, launching a half-court shot that ended the series.
The NBA's bubble season in 2020, played at Walt Disney World in Orlando due to the COVID-19 pandemic, saw the Los Angeles Lakers win the championship.
The NBA bubble was a self-contained campus where players, coaches, and staff lived and played without fans for months. It was widely praised for its safety protocols and competitive integrity.
Jimmy Butler's performances in the 2020 NBA Finals for the Miami Heat, including a 40-point triple-double in Game 3, were among the greatest underdog efforts in Finals history.
The NBA's revenue exceeded $10 billion for the first time in the 2021–22 season, driven by television contracts, merchandise, and international expansion.
NBA jerseys feature ads from corporate sponsors starting in the 2017–18 season, with small sponsor patches appearing on the upper left chest area.
The NBA's City Edition jerseys, introduced in 2017, allow teams to create unique uniform designs that pay tribute to their city's culture, history, or neighborhoods.
Basketball courts at the NBA level have various markings including the three-point arc, free-throw line, restricted area arc, center circle, and division line.
The restricted area arc is a 4-foot semicircle under the basket where defensive players cannot draw charging fouls. It was introduced to reduce collisions under the basket.
A technical foul in the NBA results in one free throw for the opposing team. Two technical fouls in a game result in automatic ejection.
Flagrant fouls are divided into Flagrant 1 (unnecessary contact) and Flagrant 2 (unnecessary and excessive contact). A Flagrant 2 results in automatic ejection.
Rasheed Wallace holds the NBA record for most technical fouls in a single season with 41 during the 2000–01 season, leading to rule changes limiting technicals.
The NBA introduced a rule limiting players to 16 technical fouls per season before facing automatic suspensions, partly in response to Rasheed Wallace's frequent outbursts.
The 'Hack-a-Shaq' strategy has been used against several poor free-throw shooters, including DeAndre Jordan, Dwight Howard, Andre Drummond, and Ben Simmons.
The NBA's overtime period is five minutes long. The longest NBA game featured six overtime periods, with the final score being Indianapolis Olympians 75, Rochester Royals 73 in 1951.
A buzzer-beater is a shot that goes in as time expires in a quarter or game. Some of the most famous include Michael Jordan's shot over Craig Ehlo in 1989 and Kawhi Leonard's 2019 bounce.
Michael Jordan's shot over Craig Ehlo in the 1989 NBA playoffs, known simply as 'The Shot,' is one of basketball's most iconic moments. Jordan's fist-pumping celebration afterward became legendary.
Derek Fisher's 0.4-second shot for the Lakers against the Spurs in the 2004 Western Conference Semifinals seemed physically impossible and remains one of the most debated and replayed shots.
Steph Curry's unanimous MVP season in 2016 saw him average 30.1 points while shooting 45.4% from three, arguably the greatest individual shooting season in NBA history.
James Harden's 2018–19 season saw him average 36.1 points per game, the highest scoring average since Michael Jordan's 37.1 in 1986–87.
Russell Westbrook averaged a triple-double for three consecutive seasons (2017–20) with the Oklahoma City Thunder, a feat once considered impossible in the modern era.
The 'Hack-a-Shaq' rule changes in 2016 made intentional fouling of poor free-throw shooters away from the ball a two-shot penalty in the last two minutes of each quarter.
The NBA's average game attendance exceeds 17,000 fans per game, making it one of the best-attended professional sports leagues in the world.
The loudest NBA arena is often debated, but Oracle Arena in Oakland (home of the Warriors before Chase Center) was commonly cited for its deafening crowd noise.
Chase Center in San Francisco opened in 2019 as the new home of the Golden State Warriors, replacing Oracle Arena across the bay in Oakland.
The 'seventh man' or 'sixth man' concept refers to the home crowd's influence on games. Teams typically win more games at home, with NBA home teams winning about 60% of the time historically.
The NBA's traveling rule states that a player can take two steps after picking up their dribble. The 'gather step,' clarified in 2019, effectively gives players an extra step.
The 'Euro step' takes advantage of the gather step rule, allowing players to take two steps in different directions after gathering the ball, creating deceptive moves to the basket.
Double dribbling occurs when a player dribbles the ball, stops, and then dribbles again. It is a violation that results in a turnover.
A backcourt violation occurs when the offensive team crosses half court and then brings the ball back behind the half-court line. This results in a turnover.
The NBA's 'continuation' rule allows players to complete a shooting motion even after being fouled, as long as the shooting motion was started before the foul.
The 'Eurostep' was not widely used in the NBA until Manu Ginobili brought it from European basketball. It is now one of the most common moves used by guards and forwards.
A 'pick and roll' (or screen and roll) is one of basketball's most fundamental offensive plays, where one player sets a screen for the ball handler and then rolls to the basket.
The pick and roll became the NBA's most used play, accounting for a significant percentage of offensive possessions, with John Stockton and Karl Malone being its most famous practitioners.
The 'triangle offense,' employed by Phil Jackson and assistant coach Tex Winter, was the system that powered both the Chicago Bulls and Los Angeles Lakers dynasties.
The 'motion offense' emphasizes player movement, cutting, and ball movement to create open shots, as opposed to set plays. It has been used successfully by coaches like Gregg Popovich.
The 'Princeton offense,' a system of back-door cuts and ball movement, was famously used by Princeton University and later adapted by NBA teams.
The 'Seven Seconds or Less' Phoenix Suns, coached by Mike D'Antoni and led by Steve Nash, revolutionized NBA offense with their fast-paced, three-point-heavy style in the mid-2000s.
Steve Nash won back-to-back MVP awards in 2005 and 2006, leading the Phoenix Suns' revolutionary fast-paced offense despite not being the most physically gifted athlete.
The box-and-one defense, a junk defense mixing zone and man-to-man concepts, was famously used by the Toronto Raptors in the 2019 Finals to contain Stephen Curry.
The full-court press is a defensive strategy where a team applies pressure for the entire length of the court. It was famously used by Rick Pitino's Kentucky teams and the 2015 Warriors.
Elvin Hayes, selected first overall in the 1968 NBA Draft, scored 27,313 career points and was known as 'The Big E.' He won a championship with the Washington Bullets in 1978.
Bob McAdoo won three consecutive scoring titles from 1974 to 1976 and later won two championships as a role player with the Los Angeles Lakers.
Moses Malone, one of the greatest rebounders in NBA history, won three MVP awards and famously predicted 'Fo', fo', fo'' (four sweeps) before the 76ers' 1983 championship run.
The 1983 Philadelphia 76ers, led by Julius Erving and Moses Malone, lost only one game in the entire playoffs (going 12–1), one of the most dominant postseason runs ever.
Dominique Wilkins, 'The Human Highlight Film,' was one of the greatest dunkers in NBA history and engaged in legendary dunk contest battles with Michael Jordan.
The 1988 dunk contest between Michael Jordan and Dominique Wilkins is considered the greatest dunk contest battle ever, with Jordan winning on his iconic free-throw line dunk.
Clyde Drexler was one of the premier shooting guards of the late 1980s and early 1990s, earning the nickname 'The Glide' for his smooth, effortless playing style.
Gary Payton, 'The Glove,' is the only point guard to win the NBA Defensive Player of the Year award (1996). His tenacious defense was feared by every ballhandler in the league.
Jason Kidd finished his career with 12,091 assists, second only to John Stockton on the all-time list. He was a triple-double machine who led the New Jersey Nets to two Finals.
The Spurs' 2014 NBA Finals victory over the Miami Heat is often called the most beautiful basketball ever played, as San Antonio's ball movement dismantled Miami's defense.
Tim Duncan's bank shot was his signature move. He mastered the art of using the backboard from various angles, earning him the nickname 'The Big Fundamental.'
The NBA awards the Most Valuable Player (MVP) trophy named after Maurice Podoloff, the first commissioner of the NBA.
Wilt Chamberlain and Bill Russell's rivalry defined the 1960s NBA. They faced each other 142 times, with Russell holding a significant edge in head-to-head playoff wins.
Despite Chamberlain's individual statistical dominance, Russell's teams won the championship in eight of the years they competed against each other.
LeBron James passed Kareem Abdul-Jabbar as the NBA's all-time scoring leader on February 7, 2023, scoring a fadeaway jumper to break the record during a game against the Oklahoma City Thunder.
NBA referees typically officiate games in crews of three. They undergo rigorous training and evaluation throughout the season.
The NBA draft combine includes measurements, athletic testing, and scrimmages. Players' draft stock can rise or fall significantly based on their combine performance.
Basketball has five players per side on the court, fewer than most other major team sports, which gives individual players more influence on the outcome.
A 'double-double' means reaching double digits in two statistical categories (usually points and rebounds or points and assists) in a single game.
A 'triple-double' requires double digits in three categories and a 'quadruple-double' in four. Five-by-five games (5+ in five categories) are also tracked as rare achievements.
Andrei Kirilenko of the Utah Jazz recorded a rare five-by-five game with 14 points, 8 rebounds, 9 assists, 7 steals, and 6 blocks in January 2006.
The NBA's pace and efficiency ratings, popularized by advanced analytics, help teams and fans evaluate players beyond traditional box score statistics.
Player Efficiency Rating (PER), developed by John Hollinger, was one of the first widely used advanced statistics in basketball. Michael Jordan holds the highest career PER.
True Shooting Percentage (TS%) accounts for field goals, three-pointers, and free throws, providing a more comprehensive view of scoring efficiency than field goal percentage alone.
Win Shares, Box Plus/Minus, and Value Over Replacement Player (VORP) are advanced metrics used to evaluate a player's overall contribution to winning.
The NBA's analytics revolution, driven by the availability of player tracking data and advanced statistics, has fundamentally changed roster construction, game strategy, and player evaluation.
Moneyball concepts from baseball have been adapted to basketball, with teams like the Houston Rockets and Toronto Raptors leading the way in data-driven decision making.
The 'load management' practice, where star players sit out regular-season games to preserve their health for the playoffs, has become increasingly common and controversial.
Kawhi Leonard's load management during the 2018–19 season helped him stay healthy for the playoffs, where he led the Toronto Raptors to their first championship.
The NBA's injury report system requires teams to disclose player injuries and their likelihood of playing, replacing the old 'game-time decision' ambiguity.
ACL tears are among the most devastating injuries in basketball. Before modern surgical techniques, they often ended careers. Now, most players recover within 9–12 months.
Derrick Rose, the youngest MVP in NBA history at age 22 in 2011, suffered multiple knee injuries that dramatically altered the trajectory of his career.
The NBA has implemented concussion protocols requiring players to be examined and cleared by medical staff before returning to play after a potential head injury.
The rookie scale is a predetermined salary structure for first-round draft picks, ensuring that rookies are paid according to their draft position for their first four years.
The NBA has expanded internationally with the Basketball Africa League (BAL), launched in 2020 as a collaboration between the NBA and FIBA.
The NBA Cup (originally called the In-Season Tournament), introduced in the 2023–24 season, adds a new competition within the regular season, culminating in a knockout tournament.
Wilt Chamberlain led the league in assists during the 1967–68 season with a 8.6 average, showing his versatility beyond scoring. He deliberately focused on passing to silence critics.
Larry Bird once played an entire game left-handed, scoring 47 points against the Portland Trail Blazers, just to challenge himself because he found the game too easy.
Shaquille O'Neal holds a doctorate in education (Ed.D.) from Barry University. He has also been a deputy sheriff, a DJ (DJ Diesel), and an actor.
Kareem Abdul-Jabbar is a prolific author, having written several books on history and social justice. He received the Presidential Medal of Freedom in 2016.
Michael Jordan's career earnings from basketball were approximately $94 million, but his off-court earnings from endorsements and business ventures have exceeded $2 billion.
Michael Jordan became the majority owner of the Charlotte Hornets in 2010 and later sold the team in 2023 for a reported $3 billion.
LeBron James became the first active NBA player to become a billionaire in 2022, through a combination of salary, endorsements, investments, and media ventures.
Athletes like LeBron James, Kevin Durant, and Stephen Curry have built significant business portfolios, investing in technology companies, media production, and real estate.
The salary cap for the 2023–24 season was approximately $136 million, with the luxury tax threshold at about $165 million. These numbers rise each year based on league revenue.
Earvin 'Magic' Johnson became a highly successful businessman after retiring from basketball, building a business empire that includes movie theaters, Starbucks franchises, and investment firms.
The Phoenix Suns' seven-seconds-or-less offense under Mike D'Antoni was ahead of its time. Many of its principles — pace, space, three-point shooting — became standard NBA strategy.
The San Antonio Spurs went from drafting Tim Duncan in 1997 to winning their last championship in 2014, a dynasty spanning nearly two decades of sustained excellence.

The 'Big Three' concept in basketball involves assembling three star players on one team. Notable examples include the Celtics (Bird, McHale, Parish), Heat (LeBron, Wade, Bosh), and Warriors (Curry, Thompson, Green).
The original Celtics 'Big Three' of the 1980s consisted of Larry Bird, Kevin McHale, and Robert Parish, who led the team to three championships.
Tim Hardaway's crossover, the 'UTEP Two-Step,' was so effective that defenders would often fall down trying to guard him. Isiah Thomas called it the hardest move to defend in basketball.
The NBA has hosted regular-season games in Mexico City since 1992, making it one of the first international venues for NBA competition.
The Toronto Raptors' 2019 championship run included victories over the Orlando Magic, Philadelphia 76ers, Milwaukee Bucks, and Golden State Warriors, all in the same postseason.
Jimmy Butler, undrafted in many mock drafts, was selected 30th overall in 2011 and developed into a six-time All-Star and one of the NBA's most intense competitors.
Nikola Jokic's passing ability is unprecedented for a center. He regularly records triple-doubles and has been compared to Magic Johnson in terms of court vision.

Giannis Antetokounmpo was selling goods on the streets of Athens, Greece, before being drafted by the Milwaukee Bucks. His journey from poverty to NBA superstardom is one of basketball's greatest stories.
The Milwaukee Bucks selected Giannis Antetokounmpo with the 15th pick in the 2013 NBA Draft. He was a relatively unknown prospect from Greece's second division.
Luka Doncic's step-back three-pointer has become one of the most feared moves in the NBA due to his ability to create space against any defender.
The NBA's assist-to-turnover ratio is an important metric for evaluating point guards, measuring how efficiently they create scoring opportunities while protecting the ball.
Jason Williams, known as 'White Chocolate,' was famous for his flashy passes, including the iconic elbow pass during the 2000 NBA All-Star Weekend.
The 'Shaq-Kobe' Lakers of the early 2000s won three straight titles, but internal tensions between the two stars ultimately led to the dynasty's end.
Phil Jackson's coaching philosophy incorporated Zen Buddhism and Native American spirituality, earning him the nickname 'The Zen Master.'
Phil Jackson gave his players books to read during the season, tailored to each player's personality. Michael Jordan received books on leadership and business strategy.
The NBA's 'superteam' phenomenon has drawn praise and criticism. Fans debate whether consolidating talent on a few teams enhances or diminishes competition.
The Miami Heat's 27-game winning streak in the 2012–13 season is the second-longest in NBA history, behind only the 1971–72 Lakers' 33-game streak.
The NBA has retired several numbers league-wide: Bill Russell's #6 for all teams, and individual teams retire numbers of their greatest players.
The Boston Celtics have retired more jersey numbers than any other NBA team, honoring legends like Bill Russell, Larry Bird, John Havlicek, Bob Cousy, and Paul Pierce.
The WNBA's 'W' logo was redesigned in 2019 as part of a rebranding effort, with the league also introducing new team identities and marketing campaigns.
Breanna Stewart won two WNBA championships, two Finals MVP awards, and an MVP award, establishing herself as one of the WNBA's all-time greats.
Candace Parker became the first woman to dunk in an NCAA tournament game in 2006 and later won two WNBA MVP awards and a championship.
Sheryl Swoopes was the first player signed in the WNBA and won four WNBA championships with the Houston Comets. She also won three Olympic gold medals.
The WNBA's growth in popularity has been fueled by social media, increased broadcasting coverage, and the arrival of marquee talents like Caitlin Clark.
The 3x3 basketball format was added to the Olympics in 2021 at the Tokyo Games, bringing a fast-paced, streetball-inspired version of the sport to the Olympic stage.
In 3x3 basketball, games are played to 21 points or 10 minutes, whichever comes first. Shots from behind the arc are worth 2 points, and all other field goals are worth 1.
The basketball used in 3x3 is a size 6 ball (the same size as the women's ball) but with the weight of the men's size 7 ball, creating a unique shooting feel.
Kobe Bryant's farewell game on April 13, 2016, saw him score 60 points against the Utah Jazz, closing his career with one final legendary performance.
The concept of 'Mamba Mentality,' coined by Kobe Bryant, represents a relentless pursuit of excellence, tireless work ethic, and refusal to accept anything less than the best.
Kevin Garnett's intensity was legendary. He was known for screaming, trash-talking, and even head-butting the goalpost before games as part of his pregame ritual.
The pick-and-pop play is a variation of the pick-and-roll where the screener 'pops out' to the perimeter for a jump shot instead of rolling to the basket.
The NBA's restricted free agency system gives teams the right to match any offer sheet their restricted free agents sign with another team, helping retain young talent.
Sign-and-trade deals allow teams to sign their own free agents and immediately trade them, enabling the player to get a larger contract while giving the original team assets in return.
The amnesty clause, available in certain CBAs, allows teams to waive a player without that player's salary counting against the salary cap or luxury tax.
The NBA's trade deadline typically falls in February, creating a flurry of activity as teams make moves to bolster their rosters for the playoff push.
The NBA buyout market, which heats up after the trade deadline, allows players who are released to sign with contending teams for the remainder of the season.
Michael Jordan's competitiveness extended beyond basketball. He is known for making large wagers on golf games and card games during flights and hotel stays.

The 'Jordan Rules' were a set of defensive strategies employed by the Detroit Pistons to physically limit Michael Jordan, devised by coach Chuck Daly and executed by players like Dennis Rodman and Bill Laimbeer.
Michael Jordan's first-round playoff exit against the Boston Celtics in 1986 featured his record 63-point performance in Game 2, prompting Larry Bird to call him 'God disguised as Michael Jordan.'
The 2011 NBA lockout shortened the season to 66 games. The 1999 lockout was even more severe, cutting the season to just 50 games.
The NBA's revenue has grown from $118 million in 1984 to over $10 billion, making it one of the fastest-growing professional sports leagues in the world.
Basketball's inventor, James Naismith, was born in Canada, making basketball a Canadian invention that became America's most popular indoor sport.
The dribble was not part of the original rules of basketball. Players could only advance the ball by passing. Dribbling was introduced in the early 1900s.
The slam dunk was not always celebrated in basketball. It was considered unsportsmanlike by some in the early decades of the sport and was banned in college basketball from 1967 to 1976.
NBA teams average about 100 possessions per game in the modern era, up from about 90 in the mid-2000s, reflecting the faster pace of modern basketball.
The NBA record for most rebounds in a game is 55, set by Wilt Chamberlain on November 24, 1960. Bill Russell holds the second-highest total with 51.
Robert Parish, known as 'The Chief,' played 21 NBA seasons — the most in league history at the time — and won four championships with the Boston Celtics.
Vince Carter's NBA career spanned 22 seasons from 1998 to 2020, the longest career in NBA history. He played in four different decades.
The 'Frozen Envelope' conspiracy theory suggests the 1985 NBA Draft Lottery was rigged to give the New York Knicks the first overall pick to select Patrick Ewing.
LeBron James has appeared in an NBA-record 10 NBA Finals, spread across three different teams, underscoring his consistency and longevity as a top-tier player.
Ben Simmons, the first overall pick in 2016, famously refused to shoot three-pointers despite being 6'10" with elite athletic ability, which limited his offensive effectiveness.
The concept of 'pace and space' has dominated modern NBA strategy, emphasizing fast play and surrounding the court with shooters to create driving lanes.
The 2023 NBA Finals saw the Denver Nuggets win their first championship in franchise history, led by Nikola Jokic's all-around brilliance.
The NBA's play-by-play announcers have become cultural icons. Marv Albert's 'Yes!' and Mike Breen's 'Bang!' are among the most recognizable calls in sports broadcasting.
Kevin Harlan once called a cat running on the court during an NBA game with the same intensity as a playoff moment, creating one of the funniest broadcasting clips in NBA history.
Chick Hearn, the Lakers' longtime announcer, is credited with coining common basketball terms like 'slam dunk,' 'air ball,' 'no harm, no foul,' and 'triple-double.'
The concept of 'garbage time' — when a game is so lopsided that reserves play the remainder — is a common occurrence in the NBA's 82-game season.
Basketball's fast break is one of the most exciting plays in sports, featuring players racing down the court for easy baskets before the defense can set up.
The alley-oop was popularized in the NBA during the 1990s and became a signature play for duos like Stockton-to-Malone and later Paul-to-Griffin.
Block/charge calls are among the most debated officiating decisions in basketball, as the difference between the two often comes down to inches of positioning.
The NBA's Coach of the Year award has been given since 1963. It is named after Red Auerbach, who won the first award but ironically never won it again despite winning nine championships.
Pat Summit, the legendary Tennessee women's basketball coach, won eight NCAA championships and 1,098 games, making her one of the most successful coaches in basketball history at any level.
Geno Auriemma has led the UConn women's basketball program to 11 NCAA championships, including four consecutive titles from 2013 to 2016, establishing the greatest dynasty in college basketball.
UConn women's basketball once won 111 consecutive games from 2014 to 2017, the longest winning streak in NCAA Division I basketball history, men's or women's.
The WNBA All-Star Game has been held annually since 1999, featuring the league's best players in a showcase of women's basketball talent.
Maya Moore, a four-time WNBA champion, stepped away from basketball in 2019 to pursue criminal justice reform, helping to free Jonathan Irons, a wrongfully convicted man she later married.
Tamika Catchings, one of the WNBA's all-time greats, won a championship, an MVP award, and five Defensive Player of the Year awards during her career with the Indiana Fever.
The NBA's summer free agency period begins on July 1 each year, creating a frenzy of player movement, rumors, and blockbuster signings.
Wilt Chamberlain claims to have never been blocked in his NBA career, though official block statistics were not tracked until 1973, years after his prime.
The NBA's assist leader trophy is named after Bob Cousy, reflecting his legacy as one of basketball's greatest playmakers.
NBA players burn approximately 700-1,000 calories per game, making basketball one of the most physically demanding sports at the professional level.
The average NBA career lasts about 4.5 years, though superstars can play for 15-22 seasons. The league's physical demands and roster competition contribute to the short average career.
The NBA's two-minute report, released after close games, reveals whether referees made correct or incorrect calls in the final two minutes, adding transparency to officiating.
